design_philosophy.core_loop: Side-scrolling beat 'em up combat: advance through stages, defeat waves of enemies, collect weapons and power-ups, reach boss encounters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing limited weapon durability and health while facing increasingly coordinated enemy AI that blocks, steals items, and performs cooperative attacks
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Leading a task force to dismantle a criminal syndicate by fighting through urban and industrial environments as a personalized character with distinct combat styles
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: SoR3 doubles down on narrative depth for the series (character dialogue, cutscenes, complex plot with robot replacement theme) while simultaneously increasing mechanical complexity—weapons break, characters have asymmetric abilities (Dr. Zan converts weapons to energy), and enemy AI is substantially more tactical. The game refuses to be a pure action-button-masher, rewarding learning stage layouts, enemy patterns, and character specialization. Western release deliberately raises difficulty and removes a playable character (Ash), suggesting developer conviction that challenge should scale with region-specific expectations.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with continue system; specific count not documented
difficulty.continues_system: Present; allows progression continuation after game over
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; no mid-stage checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Four distinct endings tied to difficulty setting and time-based performance conditions on specific stages, creating replayability hooks. Blitz attack upgrades persist through successive playthroughs, allowing skill accumulation. Character selection fundamentally alters difficulty due to asymmetric movesets and weapon compatibility

progression.upgrade_system: Blitz attacks (special moves) can be upgraded through successive playthroughs; rechargeable Special attack meter grows and becomes available without health sacrifice when full; weapons have limited uses before breaking, forcing tactical resource management
progression.unlock_mechanism: Two bonus characters (Shiva, Roo/Victy) unlocked by fulfilling unspecified conditions during play; Ash exclusive to Japanese release. Unlockable characters cannot hold weapons, creating distinct playstyle constraints
replayability_hooks: Four endings tied to difficulty setting and stage-specific time challenges
Blitz attack upgrade persistence across playthroughs
Stage alternate routes based on conditional actions (e.g., clearing enemies before NPC death)
Character-specific weapon integration and moveset variations
Battle mode for two-player competitive play

world_building_techniques: Cutscenes providing plot exposition (reveals Mr. X's brain-in-capsule endgame, robot replacement conspiracy)
Stage hazards and design reflecting narrative context (forest hideout, city infiltration, bomb placement)
NPC rescue mechanics tied to stage design (saving Chief Ivan from replacement, preventing NPC poisoning)
Multiple endings contingent on player performance, suggesting narrative branching based on efficiency

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses serve as narrative anchors (Dr. Dahm as primary antagonist scientist, Robot Y as Mr. X's avatar) and represent escalating threat complexity. Dr. Zan's story integration (discovering conspiracy, triggering protagonist mobilization) suggests bosses advance plot while testing accumulated skills and weapon management. Final encounter (Mr. X brain capsule + Robot Y) combines multiple combat phases reflecting narrative scope escalation
notable_bosses: Dr. Dahm (Dr. Zero in Japan) - primary antagonist scientist
Mr. X - revealed as disembodied brain in laboratory capsule, final boss
Robot Y - Mr. X's robotic avatar, final stage encounter

faction_indicators: The Syndicate - criminal organization attempting city takeover via official replacement
RoboCy Corporation - legitimate corporate front masking illegal robotics research
Police/Government officials - targets of infiltration, some corrupted or replaced
Task force (protagonists) - ad-hoc alliance of specialized fighters united against Syndicate
danger_signals: Robot replacement of city officials (infiltration/identity crisis theme)
Strategically placed bombs around city (coordinated terrorist infrastructure)
Dr. Dahm's roboticist expertise weaponized for authoritarian control
Mr. X's remote control device enabling puppet governance
Escalating enemy AI coordination (blocking, stealing, cooperative attacks) signaling organized threat
environmental_storytelling: Stage progression through urban environments progressing to industrial/forest hideout reflects infiltration narrative
Poisonous gas hazards tied to NPC rescue mechanics (emphasizing time pressure and civilian stakes)
Weapon scarcity (limited uses, breakage) reflects resource-constrained resistance against larger criminal apparatus
Bottomless pit hazards from series legacy integrated into 'realistic' robot factory setting
